ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for aged ginger red date honey chia seed tea; mapped to closest parent class 'herbal tea beverage' (FOODON_03301710).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJL covers herbal infusions; no specific code exists for this multi-ingredient ginger-date-honey-chia beverage. Mapped to closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific herbal beverage preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 ml (300 g), equivalent to a full mug of this brewed herbal tea with chia seeds settled at the bottom.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Ginger tea, herbal infusion with honey and seeds; component values cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food database for regional beverage analogues
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for aged ginger red date honey chia seed tea exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database. Values estimated by compositional modelling: brewed ginger tea base (USDA FDC #171917), honey contribution (USDA FDC #169640), red date/jujube infusion (USDA FDC #168153), and chia seed contribution (USDA FDC #170554) weighted to approximate a diluted beverage at ~300 ml serving with ~5 g honey, ~3 g chia seeds, ~2 g red date solids, and ~1 g ginger solids per 100 ml. Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with this compositional estimate and have been retained with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Honey GI reference (GI ~55); University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for honey-sweetened herbal beverage
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for aged ginger red date honey chia seed tea. GI estimated at 55 based on: (1) honey GI of approximately 55 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); (2) chia seeds have a low GI (~1) and their soluble fibre and gel-forming properties modestly attenuate glycaemic response; (3) ginger and red date infusion contribute minimal additional glycaemic load. GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI range (<=55) per the University of Sydney classification system. GL per 300 g serving = (55 × 7.6 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3) / 100 = 12.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage — honey contributes 6.0 g sugar per 100 ml, exceeding the 5 g/100 ml beverage sugar threshold; sodium is negligible at 2 mg/100 ml
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request this tea without honey or with a minimal amount to bring sugar below 5 g/100 ml, as regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages independently raises blood pressure risk (AHA 2018); the unsweetened ginger and red date infusion would be a suitable low-sodium anti-inflammatory alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Honey-derived added sugar at 6.0 g/100 ml in liquid form; chia seed fibre (1.2 g/100 ml) provides partial glycaemic attenuation but is insufficient to offset rapid liquid sugar absorption; estimated GI 55 (low-medium boundary)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or limit this beverage due to its honey-derived sugar content (6.0 g/100 ml, GI ~55, GL ~12.6 per 300 ml serving); requesting the tea without honey or substituting a non-caloric sweetener while retaining the chia seeds and ginger would substantially improve its suitability for blood glucose management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.08 g/100 ml), negligible sodium (2 mg/100 ml), and beneficial bioactives — gingerols from aged ginger have anti-platelet and anti-inflammatory properties; chia seeds provide ALA omega-3 fatty acids; red dates contribute antioxidant polyphenols
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This beverage is broadly suitable for cardiovascular health due to its very low saturated fat, negligible sodium, and cardioprotective bioactives from ginger and chia seeds; however, the honey-derived sugar (6.0 g/100 ml) should be moderated or eliminated to avoid added-sugar-related cardiovascular risk, particularly for individuals with dyslipidaemia or metabolic syndrome.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Aged ginger (gingerols) is a clinically validated anti-inflammatory agent; chia seeds provide ALA omega-3 fatty acids; red dates contribute antioxidant polyphenols
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This beverage is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the anti-inflammatory properties of aged ginger gingerols and the omega-3 ALA content of chia seeds; honey should be reduced to minimise pro-inflammatory sugar load at higher intake volumes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aged Ginger and Red Date Tea with Honey and Organic Chia Seed is a warm, nourishing herbal beverage popular in Singapore and Chinese-Singaporean culinary culture. It is prepared by steeping aged ginger slices and dried red dates (jujube) in hot water to produce a fragrant, mildly spicy, and naturally sweet infusion, which is then sweetened with honey and enriched with organic chia seeds. The resulting drink has a warm amber colour, a gently spicy and floral aroma, a lightly sweet taste with earthy undertones from the dates, and a slightly viscous texture from the chia seeds as they hydrate and form a gel. Aged ginger (老姜, lao jiang) is prized in traditional Chinese medicine for its warming properties and its higher concentration of gingerols and shogaols compared to fresh ginger, which confer clinically documented anti-inflammatory and anti-nausea effects. Red dates (红枣, hong zao; jujube) are a traditional tonic ingredient rich in antioxidant polyphenols and natural sugars. Honey adds sweetness and trace antimicrobial compounds. Organic chia seeds contribute omega-3 alpha-linolenic acid (ALA), soluble dietary fibre, calcium, and a modest protein contribution. Per 100 ml, this beverage provides approximately 40 kcal, 0.6 g protein, 1.0 g fat (0.08 g saturated), 7.6 g carbohydrate (6.0 g sugar from honey), 1.2 g dietary fibre, 2 mg sodium, 20 mg calcium, 0.2 mg iron, and 0.8 mg Vitamin C. The estimated glycaemic index is 55 (low, at the upper boundary of the low GI range per University of Sydney classification), driven primarily by honey; chia seed fibre partially attenuates the glycaemic response. Clinically, this is a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) due to the honey content and is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension unless honey is omitted or substantially reduced. It is well-suited for cardiovascular health and rheumatoid arthritis management due to its anti-inflammatory bioactives, negligible sodium, and very low saturated fat. It is vegetarian, gluten-free, and dairy-free, but not vegan due to honey.

Is aged ginger tea with honey suitable for high blood pressure?
What are the health benefits of ginger and red date tea?
How many calories in ginger red date honey chia tea?

Can I drink ginger honey tea if I have hypertension?
What is the glycaemic index of honey ginger tea?
Is chia seed ginger tea vegan?
What does aged ginger tea taste like?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific multi-ingredient beverage exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database. Nutrient values were estimated by compositional modelling from approved USDA FDC component entries (ginger tea, honey, red date/jujube, chia seeds) and are broadly consistent with the input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7). GI is an analogous estimate based on honey GI from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Medium confidence is appropriate given the estimation basis and lack of a direct approved-source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are compositional estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries (FDC #171917 ginger tea, #169640 honey, #168153 jujube/red date, #170554 chia seeds) weighted to a diluted beverage matrix. No exact approved-source entry exists for this specific preparation. Input record values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are retained as they are broadly consistent with the compositional estimate. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this diluted herbal beverage are not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database as of 2026. 3. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reliably available for this specific diluted multi-ingredient herbal beverage in any approved source consulted. 4. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero): this is a plant-based herbal beverage with no industrial trans-fat sources; confirmed negligible per USDA FDC component data. 5. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 (naturally zero): all ingredients are plant-derived (ginger, red dates, chia seeds) or bee-derived (honey); no animal-tissue cholesterol sources are present. 6. The input record lists two portions with is_default: true for both — this is a data quality issue in the source; the 300 g portion has been selected as the canonical default serving for this record. 7. Cultural tags (Italian, Pizza, Coffee, Toast) in the input record reflect the restaurant category on GrabFood and are not representative of this beverage's actual cuisine identity; corrected to Singaporean/Chinese-Singaporean. 8. is_vegan is set to false because honey is an animal-derived product excluded from vegan dietary standards. 9. GI estimated at 55 (low — at the upper boundary of the low GI range, <=55, per University of Sydney GI Database classification) based on honey GI reference (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); GL per 300 g serving = (55 × 7.6 × 3) / 100 = 12.6. Previous iteration incorrectly labelled GI 55 as 'medium'; corrected to 'low' per University of Sydney GI classification where low = GI 1–55, medium = GI 56–69, high = GI 70+.
